draft.introduction: The Mary and Martha episode (Luke 10:38–42) contrasts active service with contemplative attention to Jesus’ teaching. It became foundational for later Christian debates about the active versus contemplative life.

translation.text: Now as they went on their way, Jesus entered a village. And a woman named Martha received him into her house. And she had a sister called Mary, who sat at the Lord’s feet and listened to his teaching. Martha said, ‘Lord, do you not care that my sister has left me to serve alone?’ But the Lord answered, ‘Martha, Martha, you are anxious about many things, but one thing is necessary. Mary has chosen the good portion.’
translation.original_text: ἐν δὲ τῷ πορεύεσθαι αὐτοὺς αὐτὸς εἰσῆλθεν εἰς κώμην τινά · γυνὴ δέ τις ὀνόματι Μάρθα ὑπεδέξατο αὐτόν

translator_notes.note: The phrase “one thing is necessary” (ἑνὸς δέ ἐστιν χρεία) was read by the Desert Fathers and medieval mystics as affirming the primacy of contemplation over worldly activity.
